channel.title: The Economist - World in Brief
channel.description: Catch up quickly on the global stories that matter

item.title: World in Brief - March 09, 2026
item.description:
Mojtaba Khamenei was named Iran’s supreme leader. He will succeed his father, Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, who was assassinated by an American and Israeli strike on February 28th. Mojtaba Khamenei’s elevation is the most defiant sign yet that Iran’s regime is still intact—he is known for his close ties to the security services. His appointment was decided by the assembly of experts, a group of 88 regime-approved clerics.

Oil prices surged past $110 a barrel, as fears grew of an extended war in the Gulf. Donald Trump called the rise a “very small price to pay”. Stocks tumbled across Asia on Monday. South Korea’s Kospi Index fell by more than 8%; Japan’s Topix by 5%. Asia will be disproportionately hit by the dwindling oil supply. American stock futures also dropped in early trading.

Israel launched new strikes on Iranian infrastructure, including oil fields. Mr Trump said that any decision to end the war would be taken mutually with Binyamin Netanyahu, Israel’s prime minister. Meanwhile, the Israel Defence Forces said it had struck more targets belonging to Hizbullah in Lebanon. Lebanon’s health ministry says 394 have been killed under Israeli bombardment.

Iran expanded its attacks across the Middle East. Early on Monday it reportedly sent two waves of missiles towards Israel. Iran’s Revolutionary Guard Corps, the regime’s praetorian guard, threatened to attack oil facilities across the region in response to Israel’s bombardment of its own energy infrastructure. Earlier a projectile, presumed to be Iranian, killed two people in a residential area in Saudi Arabia.

Britain’s foreign secretary, Yvette Cooper, defended Sir Keir Starmer’s approach to the Iran war, saying the government should avoid “outsourcing” foreign policy. Britain’s prime minister originally refused America’s request to use its bases for attacks on Iran, before relenting a little. Meanwhile Wang Yi, China’s foreign minister, called for the “immediate” end of the war. He suggested China still sought to arrange a meeting between Mr Trump and Xi Jinping.

Germany’s chancellor, Friedrich Merz, and his Christian Democrats suffered a blow as the Greens won a close-fought regional election in Baden-Württemberg. The CDU had for years enjoyed a big polling lead in the south-western state, but this time were stymied by Mr Merz’s unpopularity, and an uninspiring local candidate. The hard-right Alternative for Germany came third, 12 points behind the leading parties.

Voters in Switzerland chose to end the so-called “marriage penalty” in the country’s tax system. The rule requires married couples to file a joint tax return, often forcing them into higher bands than their unmarried counterparts. More than half of voters in Sunday’s referendum chose to scrap it. They also rejected proposed cuts to the licence fee used to fund Switzerland’s public broadcaster.

Follow our coverage of the war in Iran.

The Iran war becomes a test of wills

Many residents of Tehran spent Sunday huddled indoors. An overnight Israeli strike on fuel-storage depots left a cloud of toxic black smoke over the capital. The war between America, Israel and Iran, now in its second week, is only intensifying. Iran has continued to fire missiles and drones at Gulf states, despite the president’s pledge to halt such attacks. One drone hit a desalination plant in Bahrain; Saudi air defences shot down another 21 aimed at a major oilfield.

America’s president, Donald Trump, has called for Iran’s “unconditional surrender”, a demand roundly mocked by Iranian leaders. Meanwhile, the Assembly of Experts, a body of regime-approved clerics in Iran, named Mojtaba Khamenei as Iran’s supreme leader. He will replace his father, Ali, who was killed in an Israeli strike last month. Before the announcement, Mr Trump warned that the elder Khamenei’s successor may meet the same fate: “If he doesn’t get approval from us he’s not going to last long,” he said.

An unexpected turn in China’s price wars 

In fighting deflation, China wills the end but not the means. In a speech to the country’s rubber-stamp parliament on Thursday, Li Qiang, China’s prime minister, promised to bring about a rebound in consumer prices. Demand and supply will be brought into better balance, he said. The government will also discourage price wars and other kinds of “rat-race competition”.

Figures released on Monday showed that consumer-price inflation did indeed rebound in February, assisted by the late spring festival. Prices rose by 1.3% compared with a year earlier, faster than expected but still short of the government’s 2% target. Producer prices, also known as factory-gate prices, fell for the 41st month in a row. Boosting demand by enough to hit the inflation target would require a bigger fiscal stimulus than the government seems ready to offer. But if the Iran war continues to disrupt energy supplies, producer prices will get a temporary lift. China’s price wars could be offset by a real one.

Sign up to enjoy a mind-expanding mix of stories, delivered six days a week

No sign of peace between Afghanistan and Pakistan 

The war that broke out between Afghanistan and Pakistan last month shows no sign of relenting. On Saturday the Afghan Taliban, the militant group that controls the country, said Pakistan attacked five provinces in eastern Afghanistan. Pakistani officials said their neighbours targeted the border district of Khyber. Pakistan demands the Afghan Taliban renounce their support for their ideological cousins, the Pakistani Taliban, who have unleashed a wave of violence in recent years.

The strikes have been deadly. Pakistan says it has killed nearly 600 Afghan Taliban and destroyed 280 of the group’s border posts. Meanwhile, the United Nations said on Friday that 56 civilians had been killed in Afghanistan, and up to 66,000 people displaced. It added that people on both sides of the border were fleeing “heavy artillery fire”. Neither side is trying to de-escalate. Over the weekend Afghanistan’s defence minister warned: “If Kabul is attacked, Islamabad will be attacked.”

Turkish democracy back in the dock

Ekrem Imamoglu, the deposed mayor of Istanbul (pictured), appears before a judge on Monday, along with over 400 others, including municipal officials and businesspeople. It is Turkey’s biggest trial in years, and forms part of a political crackdown. Mr Imamoglu was arrested last March on corruption charges, only days before his Republican People’s Party, the country’s main opposition, nominated him as its candidate for the next presidential election, which is due in 2028 but could be brought forward to next year.

The party argues the vast list of charges against Mr Imamoglu, set out in an indictment of more than 3,700 pages, are spurious. He faces up to 2,352 years in prison, though the trial could take years. That is probably the point. Turkey’s long-time president, Recep Tayyip Erdogan, who has his eyes on another term, trails Mr Imamoglu in most polls. Mr Erdogan would be happy to have his main rival remain behind bars.

A contested legacy: the “Wealth of Nations” at 250

Monday marks the 250th anniversary of the publication of the bible of economics. Adam Smith’s sprawling “Wealth of Nations” caused a sensation in 1776. Every self-respecting economist claims to love the book, even if few have actually read it.

The legacy of Smith’s tome remains contested. It does not, as know-nothing pundits claim, encourage selfishness and greed. Smith’s views of human nature were more subtle than many of his modern interpreters assume. The “Wealth of Nations” is instead a scholarly account of how countries become rich, including through the division of labour.

Still, its novelty is overstated. Smith was far from the first to recommend free trade, predictable government policies or the division of labour as a way for countries to prosper. Is the father of economics a little overrated? Probably. Is Monday’s anniversary still worth celebrating? Definitely.

Daily quiz

We will serve you a new question each weekday. On Friday your challenge is to give us all five answers and tell us the theme. Email your responses (and your home city and country) by 1700 GMT on Friday to QuizEspresso@economist.com. We’ll pick three winners at random and crown them on Saturday.

Monday: Which was the second and last Bond film to feature Timothy Dalton?

Time is a great teacher, but unfortunately it kills all its pupils.

Hector Berlioz
